stem: A school newspaper surveyed 80 students who visited the library during lunch on a Tuesday to find out their favorite subject. Of those surveyed, 45% said math was their favorite subject. The newspaper concluded that 45% of ALL students at the school prefer math. Which of the following best explains why this conclusion may NOT be valid?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The sample size of 80 students is too small to draw any conclusion.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Students who visit the library during lunch may not represent all students at the school, since this group could have different subject preferences.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The percentage 45% is not high enough to support a conclusion about the whole school.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The survey should have asked about more than one subject to be valid.

explanation: For a sample to support a generalization about an entire population (all students at the school), the sample must be representative of that population. Students who choose to visit the library during lunch are a specific, self-selected group who may have different interests — including subject preferences — compared to the general student body. Because this is a convenience sample (not randomly selected from all students), it is likely biased, and the 45% figure cannot reliably be extended to all students at the school. The key flaw is the non-random, non-representative selection method, not the sample size or the percentage value.

distractorRationale.A: This distractor targets the misconception that sample size alone determines validity. While sample size matters, the more critical issue here is the non-random selection method. A sample of 80 randomly chosen students could support a generalization, but 80 library visitors cannot.
distractorRationale.B: Correct. Library visitors during lunch are a self-selected, convenience sample that likely differs from the overall student population in subject preferences, making the generalization invalid.
distractorRationale.C: This distractor targets a misconception that a specific percentage threshold is required for a conclusion to be valid. The percentage value itself is not the problem; the selection method is.
distractorRationale.D: This distractor introduces an irrelevant criterion. The number of topics asked about does not affect whether the sample is representative of the school population.
